title: Watery diarrhea after antibiotics

chart.oneLiner: 68F presents with several days of frequent watery diarrhea, crampy abdominal pain, and low-grade fever, two weeks after a course of antibiotics.

opening: I can't stop going to the bathroom — watery diarrhea, ten times a day, with cramps.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old woman, tired and dehydrated. Cooperative.

HPI: 4 days of profuse, non-bloody WATERY diarrhea (8–10x/day) with lower abdominal cramping and low-grade fevers. Recently finished a course of clindamycin for a dental infection and was briefly hospitalized last month (only if asked about recent antibiotics/hospitalization). NO recent travel, NO sick contacts, NO large amounts of blood.
PMH: hypertension. MEDS: lisinopril; recent clindamycin (completed). SOCIAL: lives independently.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: dehydrated (dry mucous membranes), mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 108/66, HR 104, RR 18, 38.1°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: mild diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, mild distension, no rigidity/rebound (would worsen with megacolon); bowel sounds present.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent antibiotics or hospitalization unless asked about recent medications, antibiotics, or healthcare exposure.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 18.0 with left shift (marked leukocytosis is a severity marker).
labs.BMP: K 3.2 (low from losses), BUN 30, Cr 1.5 (baseline 0.9 — AKI), HCO3 low.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute ischemia.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Hypokalaemia' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute isc … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.

ekg.imageDescription: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute ischemia.

cxr.label: Abdominal X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Here: no marked colonic dilation (a dilated transverse colon >6 cm would indicate toxic megacolon).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC abdominal film that is normal/non-dilated (to contrast with toxic megacolon).
cxr.imageDescription: Here: no marked colonic dilation (a dilated transverse colon >6 cm would indicate toxic megacolon).
searchTerms: chest radiograph Here: marked colonic dilation

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: C. difficile colitis (leading,8). Other infectious diarrhea (core,3). Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (non-C.diff) (core,3). Inflammatory bowel disease (core,3). Considers complications — toxic megacolon/fulminant colitis (CANT-MISS,3). Connects recent antibiotics + healthcare exposure + watery diarrhea to C. difficile (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: difficile colitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Considers complications — toxic megacolon/fulminant colitis. Also reasonable: Other infectious diarrhea; diff); Inflammatory bowel disease; difficile.

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool C. difficile toxin/PCR (CANT-MISS,5). CBC — WBC as a severity marker (3). BMP — potassium, renal function (3). Lactate (2). Abdominal imaging if severe to assess for megacolon (2). PENALTY: ordering antimotility agents as part of management (-2 — addressed in management).

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, possible hypokalemic changes (U waves/flat T), no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (5); recognizes possible hypokalemic changes from diarrheal losses (5); no acute ischemia (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes possible hypokalemic changes from diarrheal losses
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute ischemia.
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute ischemia.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus tachycardia; possible hypokalemic changes (flattened T waves, U waves); no acute ischemia.

coreActions.item: recognizes possible hypokalemic changes from diarrheal losses

steps.label: Read X-ray
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's abdominal film. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no marked colonic dilation. CREDIT: assesses colonic caliber (5); explicitly looks for/excludes toxic megacolon (transverse colon >6 cm) (5). PENALTY: missing the purpose (megacolon screen) (-1).
criticalFindings: Here: no marked colonic dilation (a dilated transverse colon >6 cm would indicate toxic megacolon).
steps.idealAnswer: Here: no marked colonic dilation (a dilated transverse colon >6 cm would indicate toxic megacolon).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Here: no marked colonic dilation (a dilated transverse colon >6 cm would indicate toxic megacolon).

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Stool C. diff is positive with marked leukocytosis and an AKI. What is your diagnosis and severity?
steps.rubric: C. difficile colitis, severe (6). Cites positive toxin/PCR plus severity markers — WBC >15, rising creatinine (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Stop the offending antibiotic if possible (CANT-MISS,5). Oral vancomycin OR fidaxomicin as first-line (CANT-MISS,7; note metronidazole is no longer preferred first-line for non-mild disease). Contact precautions / isolation, hand hygiene with soap and water (4). IV fluids and electrolyte (potassium) repletion (4). AVOID antimotility agents (3). Surgical consult / colectomy for fulminant disease or toxic megacolon (2). PENALTY: loperamide/antimotility agents (megacolon risk) (-3); choosing metronidazole first-line for severe disease (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Plan: Stop the offending antibiotic if possible.

criticalActions.item: Stop the offending antibiotic if possible

requiredActions: Stop the offending antibiotic if possible

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers questions asked without volunteering diagnostic clues
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked

Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68F presents with several days of frequent watery diarrhea, crampy abdominal pain, and low-grade fever, two weeks after a course of antibiotics. The diagnosis is C. difficile colitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by C. difficile colitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to C. difficile colitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
